posts.title: How to Choose the Right Riding Jacket for Malaysian Weather
posts.body:
Malaysia's riding conditions are unlike anywhere else. You're dealing with 33 °C heat, 80 % humidity, and rain that can appear in 10 minutes flat — often on the same ride. Most riding jackets on the market are engineered for European touring, which means they're designed to keep you warm. That's the last thing you need on the NKVE at noon.

Why Mesh Panels Matter

A quality mesh riding jacket for Malaysian conditions should have large perforated panels across the chest, back, and sleeves. Look for at least 40 % mesh surface area. This allows airflow even when filtering through slow-moving traffic. Without proper ventilation, heat exhaustion becomes a real risk on rides longer than 45 minutes in standstill traffic.

Avoid leather for everyday commuting — it offers excellent abrasion resistance but zero breathability. Save leather for shorter weekend rides where aesthetics and track-level protection take priority.

CE Armour: What the Ratings Mean

All protective inserts in riding jackets are rated by CE (Conformité Européenne) standards. There are two main levels:

  • Level 1 — Meets basic impact transmission requirements. Suitable for urban commuting and casual rides.
  • Level 2 — Higher protection. Recommended for highway speeds and regular long-distance riding.

Always check that the jacket includes CE-rated shoulder and elbow armour as standard. Back protectors are often sold separately — invest in at least a Level 1 back pad from day one.

What to Look for in a Malaysian Riding Jacket

Here's the shortlist when you're buying:

  1. Mesh or textile construction (not leather for daily use)
  2. CE Level 1 or higher shoulder + elbow armour included
  3. Back protector pocket (even if the protector is sold separately)
  4. Hi-vis or reflective detailing for night riding safety
  5. Removable thermal liner for rare cool mornings
  6. Proper wrist cuffs that seal against wind at speed

The Syndicate Approach

Every Syndicate riding jacket is built and tested in Malaysian conditions. We ride in our own gear, on Malaysian roads, in Malaysian weather — so when we sign off on a design, we know it works here.

posts.title: Syndicate Vol.3 Drop — Behind the Design Process
posts.body:
Vol.3 almost didn't happen the way you see it now. The original concept was cleaner — minimal graphic work, tonal colourways, simple branding. Then the moodboard session happened, and everything shifted.

The Moodboard That Changed Everything

We pinned references from Japanese moto-culture, early 2000s streetwear, and traditional Malaysian craft patterns. What came out was bold graphic language layered over a technical base. We wanted the tee to feel like wearable culture, not just branded apparel.

From Sketch to Sample

The main graphic went through six revisions. The first sample had the wrong fabric weight — 180 gsm when we needed 220 gsm. The second sample nailed the weight but the shoulder seam was off by 8mm. Third sample: approved.

The Colourways

Vol.3 launched in three colourways: Midnight Black, Bone White, and Slate Grey. Bone White was a limited run — 80 pieces only. If you missed it, black and slate are still available. Vol.4 is already in concept.

posts.title: How to Style a Rider Tee Off the Bike
posts.body:
The best rider tees don't look like rider tees when you want them to. A good motorcycle tee should work at the mamak, at a meetup, or on the way back from a weekend ride.

The Tuck Decision

Full tuck, half tuck, or no tuck — Syndicate tees are cut with a longer back hem specifically for the half tuck. For full untucked: pair with slim joggers in neutral tones.

Layering for Malaysian Weather

An open overshirt or lightweight coach jacket works well over a Syndicate tee. Keep the overshirt neutral so the graphic reads clearly.

Footwear Pairings

  • Riding boots off the bike — straight-leg denim or cargo pants
  • Clean low-top sneakers — white or black, avoid chunky trainers
  • Slip-ons or loafers — clean with tapered trousers

posts.title: Riding Gloves Explained: CE Rating, Fit, and Heat Management
posts.body:
Riding gloves are the most overlooked piece of protective gear. Your hands are the first thing to hit the tarmac in a fall — they need proper protection.

CE Ratings for Gloves

Gloves are rated under EN 13594: Level 1 (basic, urban) and Level 2 (enhanced, track/highway). If it has no CE mark, it's fashion, not protection.

Fit: What Correct Fit Means

A proper fit is snug across the knuckles with no bunching. No skin should be exposed between glove and jacket sleeve. If between sizes, go smaller — gloves pack out with use.

Heat Management in Malaysia

Choose gloves with large perforated panels across the back of the hand. Full leather summer gloves are an oven after 20 minutes in traffic. Textile or hybrid with mesh inserts manage heat best.

posts.title: Muster KL 2025 — The Syndicate Recap
posts.body:
Muster KL is the biggest Malaysian riding community gathering each year. 2025 was the largest yet — over 300 riders across cafe racers, adventure tourers, and nakeds.

The Syndicate Booth

We ran a two-day pop-up and sold out of Vol.2 tees by midday Saturday. We emergency-called our courier for Sunday restock. Next year: larger space, earlier inventory.

What We Noticed

Malaysian riders want apparel that represents the culture authentically — not international brands with a local distributor. That's exactly why Syndicate exists.

Community First

We brought a polaroid camera and went through four packs of film. The photos are going up at the showroom. See you at Muster KL 2026.

posts.title: Designing the Series 01 Jacket — From Sketch to Production
posts.body:
The Series 01 jacket almost didn't happen. The original brief: a CE-rated mesh riding jacket under RM 800. Twelve months later it cost 40 % more to produce than planned and went through more revisions than any product in our history. Worth every one.

Why the First Sample Failed

Seven critical issues: shoulder armour pocket 15mm too low, mesh seam pulling under stress, back vent zip sitting on the spine. We sent a 22-point revision list to the factory.

Sample Two: Getting Closer

The armour placement and vent zip were fixed. New issues: collar too stiff, cuff velcro too narrow, internal mesh colour showing through. Third revision.

Signing Off

Sample three passed every test. CE certification cleared. First batch: 150 units. Sold out in 11 days. Series 02 is in development.

posts.title: Why We Started Syndicate — And What We're Building Towards
posts.body:
Syndicate started from a simple frustration: we couldn't find motorcycle apparel made for how Malaysian riders actually live. Everything was either international touring gear or fashion with no protective purpose.

The Gap in the Market

Malaysian riding culture is distinctive. We ride year-round. We commute in gear. We eat mamak in our riding jackets. The apparel that existed was either purely functional or purely fashion. We wanted both.

What's Next

Short term: Expand the core collection across all categories — jackets, tees, pants, gloves, headwear. Medium term: Physical presence — pop-ups at every major event, eventually a showroom. Long term: Make Syndicate synonymous with Malaysian riding culture. Built right, with the community, from the start.
